items.title: Operation Poisson – Analyzing a Cybercriminal’s Entire Operation
items.description: A comprehensive analysis of 339 commands issued by a French-speaking threat actor nicknamed 'Poisson' over 33 days, targeting a French automotive small business and four French individuals. The attacker utilized a multi-stage fileless attack deploying a 70-line Python keylogger to harvest banking and email credentials. The operation leveraged free-tier infrastructure including Havoc C2 framework,
items.ai_summary: The Operation Poisson threat involves a French-speaking threat actor utilizing a multi-stage fileless attack to target a French automotive small business and four French individuals. The attacker deployed a 70-line Python keylogger to harvest banking and email credentials, leveraging the Havoc C2 framework and free-tier infrastructure. The attack vector involved input capture (T1056) and application layer protocol (T1071) tactics, with the threat actor using multi-stage channels (T1104) to maintain access. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust input validation and monitoring, as well as restrict access to sensitive applications and data. Additionally, defenders should update their security controls to detect and block suspicious Python scripts and Havoc C2 framework activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1056| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1104| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Popular node-ipc npm Package Infected with Credential Stealer
items.description: A supply chain attack has compromised the node-ipc npm package, with malicious versions 9.1.6, 9.2.3, and 12.0.1 containing obfuscated stealer and backdoor functionality. The attack vector involved takeover of a dormant maintainer account through an expired email domain. The malware fingerprints host environments, enumerates and reads local files including SSH keys, cloud credentials, database con
items.ai_summary: The node-ipc npm package has been compromised in a supply chain attack, with versions 9.1.6, 9.2.3, and 12.0.1 containing malicious code that steals credentials and establishes a backdoor. The attack vector involved the takeover of a dormant maintainer account through an expired email domain, allowing the threat actor to inject obfuscated malware into the package. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to the theft of sensitive information, including SSH keys, cloud credentials, and database connections, affecting developers and organizations that rely on the compromised package. To remediate, defenders should immediately remove the affected versions and update to a secure version, such as 10.0.0 or later, and monitor their environments for signs of malicious activity. Additionally, developers should verify the integrity of their dependencies and consider implementing package signing and verification mechanisms to prevent similar attacks in the future.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    C -->|T1592| D[Gather Victim Host Info]:::tactic
    D -->|T1078.004| E[Cloud Accounts]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Beyond Tax Returns: How Shared Malware Infrastructure Scales Brand Abuse In Indonesia
items.description: A sophisticated fraud campaign exploiting Indonesia's tax season targeted 67 million residents through fake Coretax applications distributed via phishing websites and WhatsApp social engineering. The GoldFactory threat cluster orchestrated operations using Gigabud.RAT and MMRat malware families with shared infrastructure abusing over 16 trusted brands across government and financial sectors. The a
items.ai_summary: The GoldFactory threat cluster has been orchestrating a sophisticated fraud campaign in Indonesia, targeting 67 million residents through fake Coretax applications distributed via phishing websites and WhatsApp social engineering, exploiting the trust in over 16 government and financial sector brands. The attackers utilized Gigabud.RAT and MMRat malware families, leveraging shared infrastructure to scale their operations. If exploited, victims may experience unauthorized access to sensitive information, financial loss, and compromised personal data. To remediate, defenders should implement robust phishing detection and prevention measures, such as multi-factor authentication and regular security awareness training for employees, and ensure all systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches. Additionally, monitoring for suspicious activity related to the Gigabud.RAT and MMRat malware families is crucial to detect and respond to potential incidents.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1055| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1082| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation Dragon Whistle: UNG002 Targets Chinese Academia via Weaponized Institutional Lure
items.description: A sophisticated spear-phishing campaign designated Operation Dragon Whistle has been identified targeting Changzhou University in China. The threat actor UNG002 leveraged highly contextual social engineering by impersonating official university communications regarding mandatory 2026 National Student Physical Fitness and Health Standards testing, which directly impacts graduation eligibility. The
items.ai_summary: Operation Dragon Whistle is a sophisticated spear-phishing campaign targeting Changzhou University in China, leveraging highly contextual social engineering by impersonating official university communications. The threat actor UNG002 uses phishing emails (T1566) to trick victims into executing malicious files (T1204), potentially leading to unauthorized access to sensitive information. If exploited, this could result in the theft of personal data, intellectual property, or other confidential information, directly impacting students' graduation eligibility. To remediate, defenders should implement email filters to block suspicious emails, educate users about phishing attacks, and enforce multi-factor authentication for all university accounts. Additionally, users should be cautious when receiving emails with attachments or links from unfamiliar senders, especially those claiming to be from official university sources.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1055| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1531| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: PureLogs: Delivery via PawsRunner Steganography
items.description: Attackers are concealing .NET infostealers within seemingly innocuous images to evade detection. A phishing campaign uses TXZ archive attachments with invoice-themed lures to initiate infection. The embedded JavaScript leverages environment variables to hide malicious commands, launching PowerShell to decode and decrypt payloads. PawsRunner, a steganography loader, extracts encrypted data from PNG
items.ai_summary: The PureLogs threat involves a phishing campaign that uses TXZ archive attachments with invoice-themed lures to initiate infection, leveraging environment variables to hide malicious commands. The attackers conceal .NET infostealers within seemingly innocuous images using PawsRunner steganography to evade detection. If exploited, this threat can lead to the exfiltration of sensitive information, with potential targets including individuals and organizations that handle financial transactions. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement email filters to block TXZ archive attachments and restrict the execution of PowerShell scripts, particularly those that utilize obfuscated commands. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity related to the deobfuscation and decoding of files, and update their security software to detect the PawsRunner steganography loader.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1027| D[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    D -->|T1140| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: The Evolution of ClickFix: From Cleartext to Server Side Polymorphism
items.description: The ClickFix campaign has evolved from basic disk-based infections to sophisticated, obfuscated attacks using fake CAPTCHA pages that trick victims into executing malicious PowerShell commands. Initial variants used cleartext commands downloading batch scripts to deploy DeerStealer InfoStealer. The campaign advanced to fileless execution using XOR encryption or Base64 compression, operating entire
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix campaign has evolved from basic disk-based infections to sophisticated, obfuscated attacks using fake CAPTCHA pages that trick victims into executing malicious PowerShell commands. Initial variants used cleartext commands downloading batch scripts to deploy DeerStealer InfoStealer. The campaign advanced to fileless execution using XOR encryption or Base64 compression, operating entirely in memory. This allows the attackers to evade detection and maintain persistence on compromised systems. To remediate, defenders should ensure PowerShell is not running with elevated privileges, and implement a least privilege model to limit the attack surface. Additionally, implement a robust security information and event management (SIEM) system to detect and alert on suspicious PowerShell activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1027| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: The Gentleman Ransomware | Defense Evasion TTPs Uncovered
items.description: In April and May 2026, investigations revealed two incidents involving The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service operation, which has claimed over 400 victims across 70 countries since mid-2025. Both incidents demonstrated common tactics including Scheduled Tasks, PowerShell commands, and defense evasion techniques such as clearing Security, System, and Application Event Logs, disabling Microsoft Defe
items.ai_summary: The Gentleman ransomware-as-a-service operation has been linked to over 400 victims across 70 countries since mid-2025. Investigations in April and May 2026 revealed two incidents involving this threat actor, which employed common tactics including Scheduled Tasks, PowerShell commands, and defense evasion techniques. Specifically, the threat actor used T1588 (Obtain Capabilities) to gather necessary tools, T1053 (Scheduled Task/Job) to execute malicious tasks, and T1059.001 (PowerShell) to execute malicious PowerShell commands. To evade detection, the threat actor cleared Security, System, and Application Event Logs using T1562 (Impair Defenses) and disabled Microsoft Defender using T1486 (Data Encrypted for Impact). To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that Security, System, and Application Event Logs are not cleared, and Microsoft Defender is enabled, specifically by configuring the Windows Event Log settings to prevent log clearing and ensuring that Microsoft Defender is running and updated to the latest version.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1588| B[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1053| C[Scheduled Task/Job]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[PowerShell]:::tactic
    D -->|T1562| E[Impair Defenses]:::tactic
    E -->|T1486| F[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: One Man, One AI, One Fake Persona: Inside the 5-Year Influence and Fraud 'Patriot Bait' Campaign
items.description: A solo Russian-speaking threat actor tracked as 'bandcampro' operated a five-year MAGA-themed Telegram channel with approximately 17,000 subscribers, initially forwarding cryptocurrency scam content before pivoting to AI-automated operations in September 2025. The actor utilized jailbroken Google Gemini to generate QAnon-styled posts, deploy infrastructure, manage stolen API keys, and run credenti
items.ai_summary: A Russian-speaking threat actor, tracked as 'bandcampro,' operated a five-year MAGA-themed Telegram channel with approximately 17,000 subscribers, initially forwarding cryptocurrency scam content before pivoting to AI-automated operations in September 2025. The actor utilized jailbroken Google Gemini to generate QAnon-styled posts, deploy infrastructure, manage stolen API keys, and run credential phishing campaigns. This campaign, dubbed 'Patriot Bait,' demonstrates the threat actor's ability to adapt and leverage AI to automate operations, increasing their efficiency and reach. To remediate, users should be cautious of unsolicited Telegram messages, especially those containing cryptocurrency scams or QAnon-styled content. Additionally, users should ensure their Google Gemini accounts are not jailbroken and implement multi-factor authentication to prevent API key misuse.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Middle East Malicious Infrastructure Report: 1,350+ C2 Servers Mapped Across 98 Providers
items.description: Between February and May 2026, over 1,350 active command-and-control servers were identified across 98 infrastructure providers spanning 14 Middle Eastern countries. Saudi Arabia's STC hosted 981 C2 servers, representing 72.4% of all regional malicious infrastructure, the largest concentration globally. C2 infrastructure dominated at 96.8% of detected activity, with IoT-focused botnets like Hajime
items.ai_summary: A sophisticated threat actor has been identified to have established a vast network of over 1,350 command-and-control (C2) servers across 98 infrastructure providers in 14 Middle Eastern countries. The majority of these servers, approximately 981, were found to be hosted by Saudi Arabia's STC, representing 72.4% of the regional malicious infrastructure. This C2-dominated activity, comprising 96.8% of detected activity, includes IoT-focused botnets like Hajime. To remediate this threat, defenders should prioritize monitoring for suspicious network activity, particularly from IoT devices, and implement robust security measures to prevent unauthorized access to C2 servers. Specifically, organizations should ensure that their infrastructure providers are using secure protocols, such as HTTPS, and regularly update their systems to the latest versions, including STC's servers.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583| B[Acquire Infrastructure]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Cloud Atlas activity in the second half of 2025 and early 2026: new tools and a new payload
items.description: Cloud Atlas APT group targeted government organizations and commercial companies in Russia and Belarus during late 2025 and early 2026, employing phishing campaigns with malicious ZIP archives containing LNK shortcuts. The attackers deployed multiple backdoors including VBCloud for file theft and PowerShower for network reconnaissance. New tools identified include PowerCloud, which exfiltrates dat
items.ai_summary: The Cloud Atlas APT group targeted government organizations and commercial companies in Russia and Belarus through phishing campaigns with malicious ZIP archives containing LNK shortcuts. The attackers deployed multiple backdoors, including VBCloud for file theft and PowerShower for network reconnaissance. New tools identified include PowerCloud, which exfiltrates data. If exploited, this vulnerability allows attackers to gather victim network info, conduct phishing, archive files via utility, use remote access software, exfiltrate data over C2 channels, and encrypt data for impact. To remediate, organizations should update their email filters to block suspicious ZIP archives, implement anti-phishing measures, and ensure all software is up-to-date, particularly PowerShower and PowerCloud.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1560.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    E -->|T1486| F[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-53812: OpenClaw before 2026.5.18 contains a server-side request forgery vulnerability i...
items.description: OpenClaw before 2026.5.18 contains a server-side request forgery vulnerability in browser control that allows authenticated users to bypass private-network navigation checks through Playwright act interactions. Attackers can trigger navigation to private-network targets via action-triggered redirects and subsequently read restricted page content using browser evaluation capabilities.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-53812 is a server-side request forgery (SSRF) vulnerability in OpenClaw before 2026.5.18, allowing authenticated users to bypass private-network navigation checks. Attackers can exploit this vulnerability by triggering navigation to private-network targets via action-triggered redirects and subsequently read restricted page content using browser evaluation capabilities. This vulnerability is exploitable through the Playwright act interactions feature, which is a publicly-facing application. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive information. To remediate this vulnerability, update OpenClaw to version 2026.5.18 or later, and ensure that the Playwright act interactions feature is disabled or restricted to authorized users.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-53811: OpenClaw before 2026.5.7 contains a privilege escalation vulnerability in the Ma...
items.description: OpenClaw before 2026.5.7 contains a privilege escalation vulnerability in the Matrix allowFrom feature that allows authenticated accounts to match policy entries through mutable display name metadata. Attackers with the ability to change display names can receive agent access intended for another Matrix identity, potentially gaining unauthorized permissions depending on operator configuration.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-53811 is a privilege escalation vulnerability in OpenClaw before 2026.5.7, specifically in the Matrix allowFrom feature. Attackers can exploit this vulnerability by changing display names to match policy entries, potentially gaining unauthorized permissions. This vulnerability affects authenticated accounts and can be exploited by attackers with the ability to change display names. To remediate this vulnerability, update OpenClaw to version 2026.5.7 or later, and ensure that operator configuration does not grant excessive permissions. Additionally, implement strict access controls and monitor for suspicious activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1068| B[Exploitation for Privilege Escalation]:::tactic

    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-53810: OpenClaw before 2026.5.18 contains a code execution vulnerability where marketpl...
items.description: OpenClaw before 2026.5.18 contains a code execution vulnerability where marketplace runtime extension metadata can redirect loading toward unscanned package payloads. Attackers with trusted operator access can manipulate extension metadata to load plugin code outside reviewed package entry points, bypassing security scanning.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-53810 is a code execution vulnerability in OpenClaw before 2026.5.18, where attackers with trusted operator access can manipulate marketplace runtime extension metadata to load plugin code outside reviewed package entry points, bypassing security scanning. This allows attackers to execute arbitrary code, potentially leading to data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate, update to OpenClaw 2026.5.18 or later, and ensure that security scanning is enabled for all package entry points. Additionally, consider implementing additional security measures, such as code signing and validation, to prevent similar attacks. It is also recommended to monitor for suspicious activity and implement incident response plans to quickly respond to potential attacks.

items.title: CVE-2026-53809: OpenClaw before 2026.4.25 contains a policy bypass vulnerability in embedded run...
items.description: OpenClaw before 2026.4.25 contains a policy bypass vulnerability in embedded runner policy that allows requests using provider aliases to compare against aliases instead of canonical provider identities. Attackers can exploit this confusion to select bundled tool access outside intended provider policy restrictions when the affected feature is enabled.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-53809 is a policy bypass vulnerability in OpenClaw's embedded runner policy, affecting versions prior to 2026.4.25. Attackers can exploit this vulnerability to bypass intended provider policy restrictions by comparing against aliases instead of canonical provider identities. This allows attackers to select bundled tool access outside intended restrictions when the affected feature is enabled. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive tools and data. To remediate, update OpenClaw to version 2026.4.25 or later, and ensure the affected feature is disabled. Additionally, review and update provider policies to ensure they are using canonical provider identities.
items.workflow_graph:

    B -->|T1210| C[Exploitation of Remote Services]:::tactic

items.title: CVE-2026-53808: OpenClaw before 2026.5.6 contains an approval policy bypass vulnerability in the...
items.description: OpenClaw before 2026.5.6 contains an approval policy bypass vulnerability in the Skill Workshop apply flow that allows agent tool calls to set apply: true despite approvalPolicy: pending configuration. Attackers can exploit this by reaching the affected apply path to apply workshop changes before the expected approval step, potentially modifying configurations without proper authorization.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-53808 is a vulnerability in OpenClaw before 2026.5.6 that allows attackers to bypass approval policies in the Skill Workshop apply flow. This enables attackers to set apply: true despite approvalPolicy: pending configuration, potentially modifying configurations without proper authorization. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to unauthorized changes to configurations, which may result in data breaches or privilege escalation. To remediate this issue, administrators should update OpenClaw to version 2026.5.6 or later, and ensure that the approval policy is properly configured. Additionally, it is recommended to monitor the Skill Workshop apply flow for any suspicious activity.

items.title: CVE-2026-53807: OpenClaw before 2026.5.6 contains an authorization bypass vulnerability in Teleg...
items.description: OpenClaw before 2026.5.6 contains an authorization bypass vulnerability in Telegram interactive callbacks that allows authenticated users to skip commands.allowFrom validation. Attackers can invoke affected callbacks to mark themselves as authorized senders before allowlist checks are applied, triggering command behavior outside configured Telegram sender restrictions.

items.title: CVE-2026-53806: OpenClaw before 2026.5.12 contains a shell option parsing vulnerability that all...
items.description: OpenClaw before 2026.5.12 contains a shell option parsing vulnerability that allows combined POSIX shell flags to bypass exec revalidation checks. Attackers can exploit this by using combined shell options to execute inline shell content without intended allowlist validation, potentially enabling unauthorized command execution when the affected feature is enabled.

items.title: AryStinger botnet infected thousands of D-Link routers worldwide
items.description: A previously undocumented malware botnet named AryStinger has compromised more than 4,000 outdated routers to turn them into proxies for malicious traffic. [...]

items.title: New Prinz Eugen ransomware prioritizes recent files for encryption
items.description: A new ransomware operation named 'Prinz Eugen' prioritizes recently modified files for encryption and leaves no ransom note on the system. [...]

items.title: Microsoft links Mastra AI supply chain attack to North Korean hackers
items.description: Microsoft has attributed a recent Mastra AI supply chain attack that compromised more than 140 npm packages to the North Korean hacking group Sapphire Sleet, also known as BlueNoroff. [...]

items.title: Hackers Exploit Gravity SMTP WordPress Plugin Bug to Expose API Keys
items.description: Threat actors are exploiting a recently patched security flaw impacting Gravity SMTP, a WordPress plugin that's installed on about 100,000 sites. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-4020 (CVSS score: 5.3), is a medium-severity information disclosure flaw that can allow unauthenticated attackers to extract sensitive data, such as configuration data, API keys, secrets, and OAuth tokens

items.description: French President Emmanuel Macron urged the world’s wealthy democracies to work together on regulating advanced AI systems. The post French President Urges US to Share Cutting-Edge AI and Democracies to Cooperate on Regulation appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Unpatchable 'usbliter8' Exploit Breaks Apple A12 and A13 SecureROM Boot Chain
items.description: Security researchers at Paradigm Shift have published a working exploit, dubbed usbliter8, that achieves arbitrary code execution inside the SecureROM of Apple's A12 and A13 chips. That code is burned into the silicon at manufacture. No software update can reach it. Affected devices will carry this flaw for as long as they stay in use. This is not a remote attack. It requires

items.description: The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS) operation is actively developing and maintaining a suite of endpoint detection and response (EDR) killers that it hands out to affiliates for impairing system defenses before deploying the encryptor. This mature portfolio of EDR-terminating tools is centered around a framework that's known as GentleKiller. "They also incorporate third-party or

items.title: Texas govt data breach exposes over 3 million driver’s licenses
items.description: The Texas Parks and Wildlife Department (TPWD) disclosed a data breach at its license system vendor that exposed personal information for more than three million individuals. [...]

items.title: AutoJack Attack Lets One Web Page Hijack AI Agent for Host Code Execution
items.description: Microsoft researchers have detailed an exploit chain, named AutoJack, that turns an AI browsing agent into a delivery vehicle for remote code execution. Steer the agent to load an attacker's web page, and that page's JavaScript can reach a privileged local service on the same machine and spawn a process on the host. No credentials, no sign-in screen, and no further user interaction once

items.title: In Other News: Apple Patches Beats Eavesdropping Flaw, DOT Closes Delta CrowdStrike Probe, AWS Continuum
items.description: Other noteworthy stories that might have slipped under the radar: Android TV botnet Popa linked to Israeli firm, Velvet Ant maintained decade-long stealth, unpatched GCP Config Connector flaw enables takeover. The post In Other News: Apple Patches Beats Eavesdropping Flaw, DOT Closes Delta CrowdStrike Probe, AWS Continuum appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Operation Endgame Disrupts SocGholish Servers, Cleans 14,971 WordPress Sites
items.description: Dutch law enforcement authorities, along with counterparts from Canada , Germany, and the U.S., have disrupted malicious infrastructure associated with SocGholish and cleaned up nearly 15,000 infected WordPress websites. "With these actions we deprive cybercriminals of access to infected computer systems," Maikel Rollman of the Netherlands National High Tech Crime Unit said. "This prevents

items.title: CISA Warns Fortinet Customers as FortiBleed Hits 86,644 FortiGate Devices
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) on Thursday urged Fortinet customers with FortiGate appliances to take steps to secure against ongoing malicious activity aimed at thousands of internet-accessible devices. The sweeping campaign, believed to be the work of Russian-speaking threat actors, has been codenamed FortiBleed. The number of compromised devices stands at

items.title: Every AI Agent Is an Identity. Most Organizations Don't Treat Them That Way
items.description: AI agents can access data, trigger workflows, deploy code, and interact with critical business systems, often with little oversight. Token Security breaks down why AI agents are becoming a new identity and governance challenge. [...]

items.title: Stressors, AI Forcing Changes to Cybersecurity Teams
items.description: As threats proliferate and AI complicates cybersecurity, CISOs say the job is getting harder, but more companies still want cybersecurity expertise, if even on a part-time basis.

items.title: Police raid malware network tied to Russia's Evil Corp hacker group
items.description: An international operation targeted the SocGholish botnet, which has been linked to the Russia-based cybercrime group Evil Corp.

items.title: Webinar: How attackers bypass MFA and how defenders can respond
items.description: Modern phishing attacks, including Device Code phishing, can undermine MFA protections and grant attackers access to corporate accounts without stealing passwords. This webinar explores how behavioral AI can help security teams detect compromised accounts faster and automate response workflows. [...]

items.title: From Assistive to Agentic: The AI Shift That's Redefining Threat Management
items.description: Introduction The average enterprise security team has 40 or more security tools, giving a lot of visibility into internal telemetry and asset data. But often, these tools are working in siloes, generating (overlapping) alerts and data. And yet, breach dwell times remain stubbornly long (~43 days), response windows keep closing before teams can act, and analysts burn out triaging noise instead

items.title: Microsoft: June 2026 Windows updates break Recycle Bin prompts
items.description: Microsoft has confirmed a confusing Windows bug that causes different filenames to appear in the confirmation dialog when deleting a file from the Recycle Bin. [...]

items.title: CryptoBandits Malware Doubles as a Backdoor, Abuses Tor
items.description: CryptoBandits uses a local SOCKS5 proxy for traffic routing, blending data theft with remote code execution. The post CryptoBandits Malware Doubles as a Backdoor, Abuses Tor appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: UK's information commissioner resigns over ‘inappropriate humour’
items.description: Writing on LinkedIn, Edwards said that while he has not agreed with how the investigation into him has been conducted, he has come to accept that his position “has become untenable.”

items.title: FortiBleed: 86,000 Fortinet Device Credentials Compromised
items.description: The large-scale credential theft campaign hit roughly half of the internet-accessible Fortinet firewalls and VPNs. The post FortiBleed: 86,000 Fortinet Device Credentials Compromised appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISA: Splunk Enterprise flaw actively exploited, patch by Sunday
items.description: CISA has urged U.S. federal agencies to secure their systems by Sunday against a critical Splunk Enterprise vulnerability that is being exploited in attacks. [...]

items.title: Forget Data Leakage: Shadow AI's Real Threat Is Access Control
items.description: The first wave of enterprise AI concern was straightforward. It was simply employees pasting sensitive data into public AI tools. Security teams responded with usage policies, domain blocks, and data loss prevention rules. That response made sense at the time. It doesn't fit the problem anymore. Shadow AI has shifted from a data leakage concern to an access control problem. The threat isn't

items.title: Cybersecurity Firms Impacted by Klue Supply Chain Attack
items.description: The hackers exfiltrated data from Salesforce instances of Klue customers, such as Huntress and Recorded Future. The post Cybersecurity Firms Impacted by Klue Supply Chain Attack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Salesforce Disables Klue App Integration After OAuth Token Abuse Exposes Customer Data
items.description: Salesforce has revealed that it disabled the Klue Battlecards app integration within its platform in response to a security incident impacting the competitive intelligence company on June 11, 2026. To that end, organizations will be unable to connect to Salesforce via the app until further notice, the American cloud-based software company noted in an alert published this week. "Salesforce took

items.title: eBanking Phishing Delivered Through IPv4-Mapped IPv6 Address, (Fri, Jun 19th)
items.description: I detected an interesting phishing email this morning. It targets a major Belgian bank:

items.title: Cisco to Acquire WideField Security to Boost Splunk’s Agentic SOC
items.description: WideField will accelerate Agentic SOC capabilities by expanding the lens on threat investigation to include identity, credentials, sessions, and blast radius. The post Cisco to Acquire WideField Security to Boost Splunk’s Agentic SOC appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: 15,000 WordPress Websites Cleaned Up in SocGholish Botnet Takedown
items.description: Law enforcement and private partners took down 106 SocGholish C&C servers and domains as part of Operation Endgame. The post 15,000 WordPress Websites Cleaned Up in SocGholish Botnet Takedown appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Apple Patches Beats Studio Buds Flaw Letting Nearby Attackers Spy via Microphone
items.description: Apple has updated its Beats Studio Buds wireless earbuds to patch a high-severity vulnerability that could be exploited by nearby hackers to eavesdrop on users. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2025-20701 (CVSS score: 8.8), refers to a case of incorrect authorization impacting the Airoha Bluetooth audio SDK that makes it possible to pair a Bluetooth audio device without user consent.

items.title: Splunk Enterprise Vulnerability Exploited in Attacks Days After Disclosure
items.description: CISA has given federal agencies only three days to patch CVE-2026-20253, which can be exploited for unauthenticated remote code execution. The post Splunk Enterprise Vulnerability Exploited in Attacks Days After Disclosure appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Novo Nordisk Breach Exposes Software Development Pipeline Risk
items.description: A leaked GitHub token underscores what most organizations get wrong: Treating secrets management as a tooling problem rather than an identity problem.

items.title: Bulgaria allowed surveillance tech firm to sell products to repressive regimes, report says
items.description: The nonprofit Human Rights Watch obtained export licensing records covering 2018 through 2023, which show the Bulgarian government allowed the surveillance firm Circles to peddle the tech to law enforcement and intelligence agencies in several countries known for human rights abuses.

items.title: Operation Escaneo Signals Shift in LatAm Threat Landscape
items.description: The threat group's curious business model may combine opportunistic monetization alongside intel collection, without much coordination between the two.

items.title: FIFA Bug Exposes World Cup Streams to Remote Takeover
items.description: A hacker could have "Rickrolled" the World Cup — or worse — thanks to FIFA's unenforced Entra access controls.

items.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
items.description: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

items.title: Majority of Internet-Accessible REDCap Servers Outdated
items.description: These servers are regularly targeted by China-linked UNC6508 for initial access and backdoor deployment. The post Majority of Internet-Accessible REDCap Servers Outdated appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Salesforce Data Thefts Continue via Klue App Compromise
items.description: Klue's Battlecards is now the third integrated application that has been compromised to steal customers' Salesforce data, and victims include Huntress, the cybersecurity vendor.

items.title: Australian sugar producer works to restore operations as ransomware group claims attack
items.description: Mackay Sugar said it was "working urgently" to verify claims that a highly active ransomware group was behind a cyberattack that shut down harvesting and milling operations.

items.title: Accenture to Acquire Majority Stake in Dragos, All of runZero, NetRise in $4.1 Billion OT Cybersecurity Push
items.description: The deal values industrial cybersecurity giant Dragos at $3.25 billion, and runZero and NetRise will operate under Dragos. The post Accenture to Acquire Majority Stake in Dragos, All of runZero, NetRise in $4.1 Billion OT Cybersecurity Push appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Get Out of Security Debt by Tackling the Exposure Problem
items.description: Teams digging out of security debt need to answer only two simple questions: Which vulnerabilities in our systems are exposed, and how long should they stay that way?

items.title: EU Gets a Head Start in Developing 6G Network Security
items.description: "Shield-6G" will combine AI threat detection, digital twins, honeypots, and more, to help carriers protect 6G networks against the threats of tomorrow.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, June 18th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9978, (Thu, Jun 18th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: The Behavior of Coordinated SSH Brute Force Attacks over the last three months [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Adam Nason, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: INC Ransomware Thrives by Mastering the Basics
items.description: And one of those basics is focusing on sectors where a ransomware disruption creates immediate pressure to pay up, like with healthcare.

items.title: Hostile states behind three-quarters of attacks on Britain's critical infrastructure, cyber chief warns
items.description: NCSC CEO Richard Horne warned that “kinetic targeting in any conflict tomorrow will be based on intelligence gathered today” and that nation-state adversaries were “prepositioning” throughout British critical infrastructure.

items.title: The browser blind spot: Why your security tool may not be blocking what you think it is [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a guest diary submitted by Varun Murdula]

items.title: Sweeping Credential-Harvesting Heist Compromises 30K+ Fortinet Devices
items.description: Attackers are actively targeting various sectors across nearly 200 countries and already have compiled a list of working credentials for tens of thousands of compromised devices.

items.title: UK Social Media Ban for Minors Has Privacy Experts Worried
items.description: The UK will ban adolescents under 16 years old from user-to-user social media platforms, despite age verification issues and privacy concerns.

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.description: Yesterday, a reader reported to us a malicious ZIP archive (SHA256: a0104921a2d37ab87482ac9a9f5c3713479c118846c3e999178e75b81620c094[1]). Once unzipped, it contains a VHDX file that discloses a malicious JavaScript after being mounted (which is automatic on modern Windows OSs):

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Evil MSI Background: BASE64 Statistical Analysis, (Mon, Jun 15th)
items.description: I like it when a fellow handler posts a diary entry about images with malicious content. Last one is Xavier: "The Evil MSI Background is Back!".

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, June 15th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9972, (Mon, Jun 15th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Friday, June 12th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9970, (Fri, Jun 12th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
